summary: Joel Spolsky responds to Dave Winer's question about why Joel on Software gets dramatically more traffic from Slashdot links than other sites. Spolsky first corrects the numbers, clarifying the difference between hits and page views, reducing the apparent gap significantly. He then explains the multiplier effect: Slashdot posts coincide with his email newsletter to 16,000 subscribers, plus dozens of bloggers linking simultaneously. Finally, he notes that deep back-catalog content keeps new visitors clicking around, inflating page view counts beyond what a single referral source would generate.
key_points: The difference between hits and page views matters enormously when comparing traffic numbers — Spolsky's 500,000 hits translated to about 120,000 page views, with only 90,000 being incremental
Traffic spikes are rarely from a single source; Slashdot links coincided with newsletter emails to 16,000 subscribers and dozens of bloggers linking simultaneously
Content depth creates a multiplier effect — extensive archives encourage new visitors to explore beyond the linked article
Readers who find content that validates their views forward it to colleagues, creating organic viral distribution
money_quotes: I'm not sure what a read is, but a hit is a single file served by the web server.
Even the simplest page on this site consists of four files: the header GIF, the Made with CityDesk GIF, the CityDesk logo, and the article itself.
I almost always get slashdotted on a day when I release a new article. This is coincidentally the same day I send email to 16,000 subscribers telling them about the new article.
Some percentage of those people say, 'Aha! This precisely proves my point!' and forward the URL to their boss or underling to hit them over the head with it. 'See? Nya!'
Joel on Software has enough old content that many new visitors stay a while and click around.
key_insight: Viral traffic spikes are rarely attributable to a single source — they result from compounding effects of multiple channels firing simultaneously plus deep content that retains new visitors.
